title: Static Context vs Dynamic Context
concept: Contrasting a fixed system prompt with a context window that is reassembled fresh on every request.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: In the Context Engineering Checklist, what did we mean by 'deduped'?

options.text: Removing repeated or overlapping chunks of information before sending them to the model
Making the system prompt shorter than 100 words
Translating the context into a different language

exercises.explain: Deduped means stripping out duplicate or near-duplicate content so the context window isn't wasted on saying the same thing twice.

cards.title: One Part Never Changes, One Part Always Does
cards.body: Every time you send a message to an AI app, some of what it 'reads' has been sitting there unchanged for weeks. Other parts were just built seconds ago, fresh for your exact question. Knowing which is which changes how you debug and design.

cards.title: Static vs Dynamic Context
cards.body: Static context is fixed text like the system prompt, written once and reused for every request. Dynamic context is rebuilt fresh each time, pulling in the latest retrieved documents, chat history, or tool results relevant to that specific request.

cards.title: A Restaurant's Menu vs Today's Specials Board
cards.body: The printed menu is static: it's the same every day until someone reprints it, just like a system prompt. The chalkboard of daily specials is dynamic: the staff wipes it clean and rewrites it fresh each morning based on what's in the kitchen, just like retrieved context assembled per request.

cards.title: A Support Bot in Action
cards.body: The system prompt says 'You are a helpful support agent, be polite, never share refund codes' — that never moves. But when a user asks about order #4521, the app fetches THAT order's shipping status right now and inserts it into the context window just for this one reply.

cards.title: Myth: Static Means 'Set and Forget'
cards.body: People assume static context is safe to ignore once written. But a stale static prompt can silently go wrong too, like referencing an old policy or a discontinued product. Static just means it doesn't change per-request, not that it's permanently correct.

cards.title: Fixed Rules vs Fresh Facts
cards.body: Think of the context window as having two lanes: one carries the same instructions every trip, the other carries different cargo every trip.

left.text: System prompt, persona, core rules — written once, reused on every single request without change

right.text: Retrieved docs, recent chat turns, tool outputs — reassembled fresh for each individual request

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the BEST example of dynamic context?

options.text: The instruction 'Always respond in a friendly tone' baked into the system prompt
The three most relevant support articles fetched based on the user's current question
A rule that says the AI must never give legal advice
The AI's name and persona description

exercises.explain: Fetching the most relevant articles for THIS specific question is dynamic — it changes every time based on what was asked, unlike fixed rules or persona text that stay the same across requests.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A system prompt is typically considered static context because it doesn't change from one request to the next.
exercises.explain: True. The system prompt is written once by developers and reused across many requests, which is exactly what makes it 'static' compared to freshly retrieved content.

exercises.sentence: Unlike the fixed system prompt, retrieved documents are part of the dynamic context because they are reassembled fresh on every request.
exercises.explain: 'Reassembled' captures the idea that dynamic context is rebuilt from scratch each time, unlike static text that just sits unchanged.

exercises.prompt: One of these three descriptions of a chatbot's context setup was written by an AI trying to sound impressive but is actually WRONG about static vs dynamic context. Spot it.

options.text: Our system prompt (static) defines tone and rules, while our retrieval step (dynamic) pulls fresh documents for each user query.
Every layer of our context is dynamic, including the system prompt, which is regenerated by the model itself before each reply for maximum adaptability.
We keep the core instructions static across sessions, but refresh the retrieved knowledge and recent chat history dynamically per request.

exercises.explain: Option B is wrong and oddly phrased AI-speak: system prompts are written by developers ahead of time and stay fixed; the model doesn't 'regenerate' its own system prompt before replying. That's not how static context works.

exercises.prompt: A team building a coding assistant has: (1) a system prompt describing coding style rules, (2) the user's current file contents fetched right before each request, and (3) a summary of the last 5 chat turns. Which statement correctly sorts these into static vs dynamic?

options.text: All three are static because they were all set up when the app was built
The system prompt is static; the file contents and chat summary are dynamic because both are rebuilt fresh for each request
The system prompt and file contents are dynamic; only the chat summary is static
All three are dynamic since the whole context window changes with every model call

exercises.explain: The coding style rules stay the same every time (static), but the current file contents and recent chat summary must be re-fetched and re-assembled fresh for each new request since they depend on what's happening right now (dynamic). Knowing which parts of your context are fixed versus rebuilt each time is key to debugging why an AI app behaves differently across calls, and it's the last core distinction in this capstone before you put the whole context engineering toolkit together.
